metadata.summary: Seven repeatable architecture workflows: design, ADR, evaluation, ATAM, migration, and review. Each is a phase pipeline with state materialized to disk.

content.title: Architecture Workflows
content.summary: Seven repeatable workflow types for architecture activities: system design, architecture review, ADR creation, technology evaluation, ATAM/CBAM assessment, migration planning (Strangler Fig), and design-document review. Each workflow is a named pipeline of steps; agents run them as role-specialized subagent chains — clarifier, designer, critic, documenter — with state materialized to disk between steps.

why.p: Architecture decisions fail most often from skipped phases (no validate step, no after-action review) and from mixing design and review in the same prompt (rubber-stamp output). Naming the workflow type forces decomposition: the system-design workflow ends with a validate phase that is a separate adversarial call, not a closing paragraph.

list.item: Standardizing repeated architecture activities across a team: pick one workflow per artifact type and stop inventing new review forms.
Onboarding: gives a new architect a checklist instead of tribal knowledge.
Multi-stakeholder decisions where the workflow ceremony (readouts, async review) is itself the deliverable.
Audit-grade documentation where you must show "we followed an evaluation method."
One-person, one-decision contexts — workflow ceremony costs more than the decision value.
Pure code-level refactors — use design-pattern methodologies instead.
Tight time-boxed prototypes — the workflows assume room for review cycles.

section.name: system-design-workflow
section.p: A complete system design workflow with six mandatory phases to prevent skipped validation steps.

rule.statement: Always decompose into exactly six phases: CLARIFY → ESTIMATE → DESIGN → DEEP-DIVE → TRADE-OFFS → DOCUMENT. Never merge TRADE-OFFS into DESIGN — the validation step requires a separate adversarial pass.
rule.rationale: Merged design+validation phases produce rubber-stamp output; the same persona that designed cannot adversarially attack it.

list.item: CLARIFY: extract functional requirements, non-functional requirements (with numeric targets), constraints, and explicit non-goals. Output: scoped spec.md.
ESTIMATE: back-of-envelope — DAU → RPS → storage → bandwidth. Output: scale-estimates.md with at least four numeric rows.
DESIGN: produce three architecture options with explicit NFR scores. Single-option output is a failure signature. Output: options.md with trade-off table.
DEEP-DIVE: detail the chosen option — data model, API contracts, critical paths (caching, queuing, DB schema). Output: design.md.
TRADE-OFFS: separate adversarial call — SPOFs, hot partitions, data-loss windows, security surface. Output: risks.md.
DOCUMENT: generate C4 L1+L2 diagrams (Mermaid), one ADR per non-obvious decision. Output: diagrams/ + decisions/.

section.name: adr-workflow
section.p: Architecture Decision Record workflow for capturing and tracking decisions over time.

rule.statement: Treat ADRs like code: versioned, reviewed via PR, lifecycle-tracked (proposed → accepted → deprecated → superseded). One ADR per decision.
rule.rationale: ADRs stored in prose wikis drift and become orphaned; Git history gives authorship, review comments, and diff trails.

list.item: IDENTIFY: flag the decision — significant, non-obvious, or with multiple viable alternatives. Trivial decisions are not worth an ADR.
DRAFT: fill MADR template — context (1–2 sentences), decision (one sentence + primary reason), alternatives table (pros/cons), consequences (positive + negative).
REVIEW: async PR review or a timed readout (10–15 min reading + 30 min discussion). Push for decision in 1–3 readouts; open-ended ADRs stall.
DECIDE: change status to "Accepted" or "Rejected." Rejected ADRs are preserved — they explain why a path was not taken.
MAINTAIN: after-action review at 30 and 90 days. Feed findings into the next evaluation round. Supersede rather than delete when the decision changes.

section.name: technology-evaluation-workflow
section.p: Structured process for comparing and selecting between technology options.

rule.statement: Force the evaluator agent to cite at least one source per claim (release notes, benchmarks, official docs). Uncited claims are hallucination candidates.
rule.rationale: LLM-generated technology comparisons frequently invent performance numbers; citations make claims falsifiable.

list.item: DEFINE CRITERIA: list weighted criteria from NFRs — latency, throughput, operational complexity, licensing cost, team expertise, ecosystem health.
RESEARCH: gather evidence per option. Use WebSearch for release notes, benchmarks, CVE history. Minimum one cited source per claim.
EVALUATE: build a weighted-score matrix. Require score differentiation — reject all-8/10 outputs.
DECIDE: recommendation must survive steel-manning the runner-up — explicitly address the runner-up's strongest argument.
DOCUMENT: write ADR with the matrix, citations, and PoC result if one was run.

section.name: atam-assessment-workflow
section.p: Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method assessment for comprehensive quality-attribute analysis.

rule.statement: ATAM utility trees from LLMs are usually too uniform — every leaf marked "high importance, high difficulty." Require an explicit ranking distribution (no more than 20% of leaves at the highest tier).
rule.rationale: Uniform trees provide no prioritization signal; the point of ATAM is to surface the top-3 risk themes, not to enumerate everything.

list.item: Step 1: Present business drivers — goals, constraints, key stakeholders.
Step 2: Present architecture — C4 L1+L2, key decisions.
Step 3: Identify architectural approaches — patterns used (caching, queuing, sharding).
Step 4: Generate quality-attribute utility tree — root QA branches (availability, performance, security, modifiability) with scenarios at leaves.
Step 5: Analyze approaches against utility tree — identify sensitivity points and trade-off points.
Step 6: Brainstorm and prioritize scenarios — stakeholder input, rank by H/M/L importance × difficulty.
Step 7: Refined analysis — address top scenarios against the architecture.
Step 8: Present results — sensitivity points, trade-off points, risk themes (top 3).

section.note: CBAM extends ATAM with cost/benefit: estimate cost for each sensitivity point, quantify benefit of each quality-attribute improvement, compute ROI. Apply only when budget decisions are at stake.

section.name: migration-planning-strangler-fig
section.p: Structured migration process using the Strangler Fig pattern for gradual system replacement.

rule.statement: Require the agent to draft the rollback plan before the cutover plan. Migration plans without rollback are incomplete.
rule.rationale: Agents skip rollback, dual-write, and feature-flag cutover unless they are required headings in the template.

list.item: IDENTIFY: select the component for migration — highest risk, highest coupling, or highest business value first.
ISOLATE: implement the replacement in the new platform with full feature parity and tests. Do not touch the legacy path yet.
ROUTE: use API gateway or feature flag to redirect a percentage of traffic to the new implementation. Start at 1%.
DUAL-WRITE (if stateful): write to both old and new stores; verify consistency before cutting read traffic.
TEST: canary at 1% → 10% → 50% → 100%. Gate each step on error rate and p95 latency parity.
CUTOVER: switch 100%, monitor for 24h, then decommission the legacy path. Rollback plan must be documented and tested before cutover.
ITERATE: repeat for the next component. Migration is complete only when the legacy system is decommissioned.

section.name: design-document-review-workflow
section.p: Lightweight review process for architecturally significant features and changes.

list.item: IDENTIFY: in sprint planning, flag architecturally significant work (new service, schema change, external API contract).
PREPARE: author circulates design.md 48h before the review meeting. Reviewers read async.
REVIEW: timed meeting — 30 min max. Focus on requirements coverage, NFR targets, trade-offs, and open questions.
FEEDBACK: written, actionable comments in the PR. No verbal-only feedback — it does not persist.
APPROVE: author addresses comments, updates design.md, merges. ADRs are merged alongside code in the same PR.

section.note: CI gate: if architecture-impacting paths change (infra/, deploy/, services/*/api/, docs/architecture/) without an ADR update, fail the PR.

section.name: architecture-review-types
section.p: Different types of architecture reviews for different purposes and questions.

list.item: Roadmap Review: answers "should we build this?" — validates business drivers, cost/benefit, and strategic fit. Timing: early planning, before any design work. Audience: stakeholders + architect.

Design Review: answers "is this design sound?" — validates requirements coverage, NFR targets, trade-offs, and implementation risk. Timing: before implementation begins. Audience: tech leads + implementers.
Risk-Storming: structured brainstorm of failure points. Each participant independently identifies risks, then consolidates. Focus: SPOFs, cascading failures, data-loss scenarios.
Security Review: threat-modeling pass (STRIDE or attack trees). Separate from design review to ensure the security lens is not diluted.
Post-Incident Review: architectural lessons from production incidents. Feed findings back into ADRs and utility trees.

rule.statement: Never combine Roadmap Review and Design Review in the same session. They serve different stakeholder audiences and different questions.
rule.rationale: Mixed sessions produce shallow coverage of both: stakeholders disengage during design details; implementers tune out during strategic framing.

section.name: workflow-selection-decision-tree
section.p: Decision tree for selecting the appropriate workflow for the current task.

list.item: New system from scratch → System Design Workflow. If complex with multiple teams → include ATAM step. If simple, single team → lightweight design only.
Evaluating an existing system → Architecture Review Workflow. If quality-attribute focus → add ATAM. If cost/benefit focus → add CBAM.
Making a significant technology decision → Technology Evaluation Workflow + ADR.
Documenting any non-obvious architectural decision → ADR Workflow (standalone).
Legacy system modernization → Migration Planning Workflow (Strangler Fig).
Sprint feature with architecture impact → Design Document Review Workflow.

section.name: llm-usage-patterns
section.p: Effective patterns for using LLMs in each phase of architecture workflows.

rule.statement: Always run the Validate phase as a separate adversarial agent call, never as a closing paragraph appended to the Design call. Different prompt, different system context, explicit failure-finding instructions.
rule.rationale: The same persona that designed the system cannot adversarially attack it; the critic role requires a context switch enforced by prompt separation.

list.item: Requirements analysis: LLM extracts and categorizes stakeholder input into functional/non-functional lists. Human validates numeric NFR targets before proceeding.
Scale estimation: LLM calculates capacity needs from business metrics (DAU, request_per_user, data_per_event). Verify formulas, not just numbers.
Design generation: LLM produces three architecture options with explicit NFR scores. Reject single-option output.
ADR writing: LLM drafts from meeting notes or decision log. Human ensures the "context" sentence is a problem statement, not a solution description.
Technology research: LLM summarizes vendor comparisons. Require citations; mark uncited claims as unverified.
Risk identification: LLM brainstorms failure modes and edge cases. Require the critic persona to produce a table: risk, likelihood, impact, mitigation, owner.
Diagram creation: LLM generates C4 Mermaid from a pasted tree -L 2 repo structure. Never generate diagrams without a repo structure reference — invented components mislead.

example.title: Design critic prompt
example.code: ROLE: design-critic INPUT: docs/architecture/specs/spec.md and diagrams/*.mmd TASK: Risk-storm the design. Produce a table: | risk | likelihood | impact | mitigation | owner | Reject mitigations that are "add monitoring" without a concrete metric+threshold.

example.title: Technology evaluator prompt
example.code: ROLE: tech-evaluator GOAL: Choose between <A>, <B>, <C> for <use case> with NFRs <...>. Output: weighted-score matrix with criteria, weights, scores, citations, and a recommendation that survives steel-manning the runner-up.

section.name: ci-cd-quality-gates
section.p: Automated quality gates for enforcing architecture standards in CI/CD pipelines.

list.item: Pre-commit: naming conventions, file organization, import restrictions (ArchUnit / Packwerk / dependency-cruiser).
PR gate: if architecture-impacting paths change (infra/, deploy/, services/*/api/, docs/architecture/) without an ADR update → fail.
Build: SonarQube (code smells, architectural anti-patterns), dependency analysis, API contract validation (OpenAPI diff).
Canary: automated smoke and load tests before full rollout. SLO parity required before advancing to next traffic percentage.

section.name: agent-decomposition-pattern
section.p: Pattern for decomposing workflows into role-specialized subagents.

rule.statement: Treat each workflow as a pipeline of role-specialized subagents: clarifier, designer, critic, documenter. Materialize state to disk between steps (spec.md, options.md, adr-NNN.md) so agents reload from files, not transcript memory.
rule.rationale: Transcript memory is lost on context reset; disk state survives and allows parallel or sequential agent handoffs without information loss.

list.item: brainstorm: diverge step in system-design and tech-evaluation workflows — generates multiple options with explicit trade-offs.
/faion: produces spec/design/test-plan triples and tracks lifecycle states against quality gates.
/faion: drives migration plan execution task-by-task with checkpoint gates.
improver: runs the after-action review 30 days post-decision against ADR consequences — checks if predictions held.

section.name: common-failure-modes
section.p: Common failure patterns when using these workflows with agents or teams.

list.item: Review workflows degrade to rubber-stamping unless the critic agent has a separate persona and adversarial prompt with explicit failure-finding instructions.
Agents emit one giant "do everything" plan; force decomposition into the canonical phases and gate each step.
ATAM utility trees from LLMs are uniformly ranked; require explicit distribution (no more than 20% at highest tier).
Migration plans skip backfill, dual-write, and feature-flag cutover; inject those as required headings in the template.
ATAM/CBAM are heavyweight; teams under ~20 people rarely sustain them. Use lightweight risk-storming for small teams.
Human-in-loop gates are non-negotiable: ADR acceptance, evaluation outcome sign-off, migration go/no-go. Wire as approvals, not comments.
Pick exactly one workflow per artifact type; stop the team from inventing a new review form per project.
For tech evaluation, force the agent to cite at least one source per claim (release notes, benchmarks). Anything uncited is a hallucination candidate.
Time-box ADR readouts (10–15 min reading + 30 min discussion) — the workflow only works with discipline.
Always run an after-action review on the top-3 ADRs at 30 and 90 days; feed findings back into the next round of evaluations.
For migration workflows, require the agent to draft the rollback plan before the cutover plan.
Convert each workflow into a CI check or PR template so it self-enforces without process police.
